**Stock Platform Chatbot Agent – Requirements & Technical Design**

---

**1\. Overview**

**Purpose**

The Stock Platform Chatbot Agent provides a conversational interface that enables authorized users to perform stock correction activities through guided interactions, eliminating manual stock correction forms and reducing operational effort.

The chatbot acts as the user-facing orchestration layer that:

* Validates user authorization  
* Captures stock correction intent  
* Guides users through data collection  
* Executes stock transactions  
* Publishes integration events  
* Maintains auditability and compliance

---

**Business Context**

Stock correction activities are traditionally performed through disparate systems and manual workflows. Common stock correction scenarios include:

* Stock Zeroisation  
* Waste Adjustments  
* Store-to-Store Transfers (STR-STR)

The chatbot provides a unified experience while enforcing business controls and governance.

**![][image1]**

**Business Benefits**

| Benefit | Description |
| ----- | ----- |
| Operational Efficiency | Reduced manual effort |
| Accuracy | Guided data collection |
| Compliance | RBAC and audit trails |
| Traceability | Event-driven transaction tracking |
| User Experience | Natural language interaction |

**Roles**

| Role | Responsibility |
| ----- | ----- |
| Store Associates/Store manager | Submit requests |
| Store Managers | Approve/high-threshold transactions |
| Inventory Operations Team | Monitor corrections |
| Supply Chain Team | Manage transfers |
| Security Team | RBAC, compliance |

---

## Phase 1 — Mock Services & Service Orchestration

Three independent Spring Boot mock services live under `services/`, one per
directory, matching the endpoints frozen in `phase-1/05_api-contract.md`.
They're mocks only: hardcoded in-memory data, no database, no auth/service/
repository layering — just controllers, per that doc's explicit scope
("just mock services... no need to build a fully functional service").

```
services/
├── auth-service/         → POST /api/login, GET /api/me            (port 8081)
├── validation-service/    → POST /api/validation/area,
│                            POST /api/validation/product            (port 8082)
├── stock-service/         → GET /api/stock,
│                            POST /api/stock/zeroization,
│                            POST /api/stock/zeroization/area        (port 8083)
├── api-gateway/           → nginx.conf routing table                (port 8080)
├── Dockerfile             → shared multi-stage build, one target per service
└── docker-compose.yml     → runs all three services + the gateway together
```

### Running all three at once (docker-compose + nginx gateway)

```
cd services
docker-compose up --build
```

This builds all three service images from the shared `Dockerfile` (one
Gradle invocation compiles all three jars; each image just copies out its
own jar — see the Dockerfile's comments) and starts a fourth container,
an `nginx` API gateway, in front of them. Only the gateway publishes a
host port: everything goes through **`http://localhost:8080`**, e.g.
`POST http://localhost:8080/api/login` — nginx routes each path prefix to
the right backend container by service name on the internal Docker network
(see `api-gateway/nginx.conf`). The three backend containers aren't
reachable from the host directly in this mode, only from each other and
the gateway.

`docker-compose down` stops and removes all four containers.

### Running without Docker

Each service is also its own Gradle subproject, sharing one Gradle wrapper
at `services/`, if you'd rather run them as plain JVM processes and hit
each port (8081/8082/8083) directly instead of going through the gateway:

```
cd services
./gradlew :auth-service:bootRun
./gradlew :validation-service:bootRun
./gradlew :stock-service:bootRun
```

Either way, there's no shared database between them — `validation-service`
and `stock-service` each hardcode their own copy of the same
store/area/product IDs (see `MockValidationData`/`MockStockData` in each
service), the way a real deployment's separate services would each read
from a shared database instead. Keep those two in sync by hand if you add
mock data.

### Orchestration: who calls whom, in what order

The Node agent's `ToolExecutor` is the only thing that calls these services
directly — they never call each other (the gateway is a routing layer, not
a participant in the business logic). Point `ToolExecutor` at one base URL
(`http://localhost:8080` via docker-compose) instead of three when the
gateway is running. The call order for a single Zeroisation request (see
`phase-1/01_phase_1_plan.md`'s end-to-end flow for the full agent-side
reasoning around each step):

1. `POST /api/login` (auth-service) → `{token}`
2. `GET /api/me` (auth-service, token in `Authorization` header) →
   confirms the employee is an authorized store manager, returns
   `assignedTo` (the `storeId` used on every call below) and `employee_id`
   (used as `requestedBy` later)
3. `POST /api/validation/area` (validation-service) → resolves an
   `areaId` from a guessed `areaName`; `AREA_NOT_FOUND` loops back to a
   corrected guess
4. `POST /api/validation/product` (validation-service, scoped to that
   `areaId`) → resolves a `productId`; `PRODUCT_NOT_FOUND` loops back to a
   corrected guess — **skip this step for a whole-area request** (see
   step 5b)
5. Either:
   - **Single product:** `GET /api/stock?storeId&areaId&productId`
     (stock-service) → `availableQuantity`, then on user confirmation
     `POST /api/stock/zeroization` (stock-service)
   - **Whole area:** `GET /api/stock?storeId&areaId` (stock-service,
     `productId` omitted) → every product currently stocked there, then on
     user confirmation `POST /api/stock/zeroization/area` (stock-service)

`validation-service` never talks to `stock-service` or vice versa — the
agent is what threads a `storeId`/`areaId`/`productId` resolved by one
service into the next call to another. This mirrors
`phase-1/02_phase_1_flowchart.md`'s "Zeroisation business flow" diagram
exactly; that diagram is the authoritative picture of this sequence,
including every retry/decline branch omitted from the summary above.

### Mock data

Seeded directly in each service's `Mock*Data` class (no config file, no
database):

| storeId | areaId | area name | storageType | products (productId / name / qty) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `STORE-101` | `AREA-10` | Refrigerator X | REFRIGERATOR | `PROD-501` Eggs (120), `PROD-502` Milk 1L (40), `PROD-503` Butter (0 — already zero) |
| `STORE-101` | `AREA-11` | Backroom Storage | STORAGE | `PROD-504` Cardboard Boxes (500) |
| `STORE-102` | `AREA-20` | Freezer Section | FREEZER | `PROD-601` Ice Cream Tub (60) |

Login credentials (`auth-service`): `priya.k` / `raj.kumar` (both valid
store managers, assigned to `STORE-101` / `STORE-102` respectively) and
`sam.t` (valid login, but not assigned to any store — exercises
`UNAUTHORIZED_MANAGER`), all with password `password123`.

### Known gaps vs. the frozen contract

Flagged in `phase-1/05_api-contract.md` and carried into these mocks as
implementation choices, not confirmed contract behavior:

- No failure-body shape was specified for bad `POST /api/login` credentials
  — this mock returns `401` with `{"errorCode":"INVALID_CREDENTIALS", ...}`.
- No failure-body shape was specified for a missing/invalid bearer token on
  `GET /api/me` — this mock returns `401` with `{"errorCode":"INVALID_TOKEN", ...}`.
- `reason` codes (e.g. `SPOILED`, `POWER_FAILURE`) are illustrative, not an
  enumerated/validated set — any string is accepted as-is.
